steps.title: Identify what would undermine the hypothesis
steps.content: Patel's hypothesis is a causal chain: otter root damage $\Rightarrow$ more sexual reproduction in eelgrass $\Rightarrow$ greater genetic diversity $\Rightarrow$ healthier meadows. The chain predicts that larger, longer-established otter populations should correspond to healthier meadows. An undermining finding would reverse that predicted relationship.

steps.title: Apply the test to the choices
steps.content:
• A: describes small/recently-reintroduced otter populations in the study — consistent with Patel's setup, not contradictory.
  • B: sites with large otter populations but no eelgrass at all — says nothing about how otter presence affects existing meadow health.
  • C: at additional sites, eelgrass meadow health correlates negatively with length of residence and size of otter populations — directly reversing Patel's predicted otter-to-health link.
  • D: involves plants unrelated to eelgrass, so it says nothing about Patel's eelgrass-specific hypothesis.
That confirms C is correct.
